our_argument_summary: The dome claims 'Solar Analemma Day Length RMS CONFIRMED (6.9 min).' Our review identifies this as misleading: 6.9 min is the RMS of the obliquity sub-component alone (amplitude 9.87 min → RMS 6.98 min), not the full equation of time (RMS ~8.8 min, range +16.4 to −14.3 min, swing 30.7 min). We note 'day length' is a misnomer (it's the equation of time, not sunrise-to-sunset duration). We point out no dome derivation exists and the dome's own solar formula borrows the globe's 23.45° tilt that produces this value (cross-ref WIN-056).

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: None. WIN-018 appears only as a one-line entry in the dome's WIN list: 'WIN-018 — Solar Analemma Day Length RMS CONFIRMED (6.9 min).' No derivation, formula, or calculation exists in any dome source file. The dome site provides no explanation of where 6.9 comes from or how it relates to dome geometry.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Entirely hardcoded. The value 6.9 is a static string. No script in the dome repository computes or validates this number. The dome's monitor.py automation (as documented in Cycle 1 review and confirmed by absence in raw-text files) does not include WIN-018 in any validated domain.
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: None — the WIN is never validated programmatically.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None. Our review correctly identifies the lack of derivation and the cherry-picked nature of the sub-statistic.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: WIN-018 appears as a static one-line entry in the dome's WIN list. No domain in monitor.py fetches analemma or equation-of-time data. The value 6.9 is never computed or checked against external sources.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: The equation of time is fully explained by standard orbital mechanics (Kepler's laws + 23.45° obliquity + 0.0167 eccentricity). The dome offers no alternative mechanism — it simply adopts a sub-statistic of this well-understood phenomenon.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: The equation of time and its components have been known since at least the 18th century. The NOAA solar equations formalize it. The dome adopted the 6.9-minute RMS value from published astronomy and labeled it 'CONFIRMED.'
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: No formula in the dome repository derives 6.9 from disc_radius, sun_altitude, firmament_height, or any other dome parameter. The dome's solar position formula itself borrows the globe's 23.45° axial tilt (see WIN-056), which is the very parameter that produces the obliquity component whose RMS ≈ 6.98 min.

holes_found.description: The detail_evidence says 'Selecting the RMS of one sub-component while ignoring the eccentricity component and the full signal is cherry-picking a derived sub-statistic.' While accurate, this could be slightly sharper. A dome defender could counter: 'We're isolating the obliquity effect specifically because that's what the dome's tilt produces.' Our text doesn't preempt this rebuttal — it doesn't explicitly state that the dome provides no derivation showing HOW its geometry produces the obliquity component specifically. The sentence about no derivation comes later in the paragraph and reads as a separate point rather than a direct counter to this anticipated rebuttal.
holes_found.recommendation: Consider tightening the logical flow so the 'no dome derivation' point immediately follows the cherry-picking accusation, making it clear that the dome can't claim to isolate the obliquity effect because it never derives ANY component from dome geometry.

holes_found.description: The finding field says 'full equation-of-time RMS = 8.8 min' — this parenthetical could be confusing to readers unfamiliar with the equation of time. The summary table row (claim + finding) is what most readers see first, and the distinction between 'obliquity sub-component RMS' and 'full equation-of-time RMS' may not land without the detail expansion.
holes_found.recommendation: No action needed — the finding is technically accurate and the detail expansion explains it fully. This is a minor readability observation, not an error.

stronger_arguments.description: The Cycle 1 review suggested emphasizing that the dome's monitor.py excludes WIN-018 entirely — meaning even the dome's own automation doesn't validate this claim. This point is still absent from the published review text. Adding a sentence like 'The dome's own 39-domain monitoring engine does not validate WIN-018 against any external data source' would strengthen the 'no derivation' argument by showing that even the dome's infrastructure treats this as decorative.

kernel_of_truth.description: The equation of time is genuine science, and the analemma is a real astronomical phenomenon. The obliquity component does have an RMS of approximately 6.98 minutes — this is a real number describing a real physical effect (the variation in apparent solar time caused by the ecliptic's tilt relative to the equator). The dome model correctly identifies this as a notable observable.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Standard astronomy derived and explained this centuries ago via Kepler's laws and axial tilt. The dome provides no alternative derivation, no novel mechanism, and no different numerical prediction. The dome's own solar formula imports the globe's 23.45° tilt — the parameter that generates this very sub-component. Claiming credit for a number you borrowed from the theory you're trying to replace is circular, not confirmatory.

deeper_analysis:
Cycle 2 assessment: All three Cycle 1 recommendations were applied successfully. The detail_evidence now correctly states the full EoT RMS as 8.8 min (was incorrectly stated as 6.9 in the pre-Cycle-1 text), identifies the obliquity sub-component origin precisely (amplitude 9.87 min → RMS ≈ 6.98 min), and notes the 'day length' misnomer. The cross-reference to WIN-056 is accurate and well-placed.

The argument is now substantially stronger than the pre-Cycle-1 version. The cherry-picking accusation is precise and backed by specific numbers. The only remaining gap is the absence of the monitoring-exclusion point (the dome's monitor.py doesn't validate WIN-018), which was flagged in Cycle 1 but not yet added to the published text. This is a minor enhancement, not a deficiency — the current text stands on its own.

Verdict stability: 'Misleading' remains correct. This is a textbook case of cherry-picking a derived sub-statistic. It's not 'Refuted by Data' (the 6.98-min RMS is a real number), not 'Self-Contradicted' (no internal contradiction), and not 'Std Model Explains' (though standard physics does explain it, the core issue is the cherry-picking, not the explanation). The verdict accurately captures the primary problem.

No new external evidence or dome site changes affect this WIN since Cycle 1.

citations_verified: NOAA solar equations PDF (https://gml.noaa.gov/grad/solcalc/solareqns.PDF) — valid URL, well-known NOAA GML resource for equation of time calculations
WIN-056 cross-reference — accurately describes the dome's borrowing of 23.45° axial tilt from globe astronomy
